mnemonics.title: Template Syntax Reminder
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'T' for Template - Think of T as Type. Syntax: template<typename T> - 'T' holds any data type!

mnemonics.title: Full vs Partial Specialization
mnemonics.content: FULL = FILL all parameters with concrete types (template<>). PARTIAL = PART of parameters remain generic.

mnemonics.title: typename vs class
mnemonics.content: T for Type - think 'typename' signals a type is coming. Use 'class' for historical compatibility - both work identically!

acronyms.usage: Provides ready-to-use class templates like vector, list, stack, queue, map

acronyms.expansion: Substitution Failure Is Not An Error
acronyms.usage: Rule allowing invalid template substitutions to be silently discarded during overload resolution
A technique where a class derives from a template parameter, enabling compile-time polymorphism
Also known as Koenig lookup, enables finding functions based on argument types in templates

visualTips.visualization: Think of a template as a STAMP/COOKIE CUTTER. When you use it with a specific type (like int), the compiler STAMPS out a concrete class/function. The stamp (template) stays the same, but each impression (instantiation) is tailored for that type!
When creating objects: Stack<int> intStack; - The angle brackets <int> are like ordering a coffee: You tell them what TYPE you want (int), and they make it specifically for you!
Think of full specialization as a COMPLETE recipe for a specific dish, while partial specialization is like a base recipe where you can still choose some ingredients (types) yourself!

analogies.analogy: Templates are like ARCHITECTURAL BLUEPRINTS for a building. The blueprint (template) describes the structure, but you need to build actual buildings (instantiations) with specific materials (types) - one for brick, one for concrete, etc.

Think of a GENERIC FUNCTION like a RECIPE for making tea. The recipe (template) says 'boil water, add leaves, strain, serve' - it doesn't specify what tea leaves. You can use black tea, green tea, or herbal tea (different types), and the process remains the same!

Unlike void* in C, templates provide TYPE SAFETY like a BORDER CONTROL at an airport. Just as each passenger needs proper identification matching their destination, each template instantiation ensures the correct type passes through compile-time checks!
